pitch_rationale: Area-weighted across 6 roof segments from Google Solar API (imageryQuality=HIGH): 18.08° → 4:12. Solar's underlying DSM model reports ~5° MAE in published benchmarks (Satellite Sunroof, NeurIPS 2024).
footprint_rationale: The subject home is the large multi-section gray hip-roofed structure inside the orange bounding box. The neighboring terra-cotta roof to the north is excluded. Bounding box estimated at ~510 px (99.8 ft) wide × ~440 px (86.1 ft) tall = 8,593 sq ft. The roof is a complex cross/L-shaped multi-section hip roof; yard is visible in the NE and SW corners of the box, so a 74% fill factor was applied → ~6,359 sq ft footprint. Ridge total (~95 ft) accounts for 3–4 ridge segments across the main body and sub-sections. Hips (~220 ft) are numerous given the all-hip construction across ~10–12 diagonal corner lines. Valleys (~65 ft) occur at 3–4 inward junctions between roof sections. Rakes (~25 ft) are minimal as the roof is primarily hip-style. Eaves (~290 ft) represent the lower perimeter of the notched footprint. Confidence is moderate due to roof complexity and satellite obliquity making precise pixel tracing challenging.

findings.description: A distinctly teal/green colored rectangular element is visible at the roof edge, clearly contrasting with the surrounding gray asphalt shingle material. It is not consistent with standard roofing material color or texture.
findings.location_description: South slope, southeast corner of the main structure, near the lower roof edge

findings.description: A noticeably darker gray patch roughly 8–12 ft in diameter on the central upper roof plane, distinct in tone from the surrounding uniform gray shingles.
findings.location_description: Upper main roof section, near the center ridge area

condition.rationale: The subject home within the orange bounding box is a large, multi-section residential structure with what appears to be gray asphalt shingles across several intersecting roof planes. The majority of the roof surface is uniformly gray with straight, intact ridge lines and no visible structural sag or large-scale missing material. Two items warranted flagging: (1) A teal/green colored element at the southeast roof edge that is visually distinct from all surrounding gray shingle material — its color and shape are inconsistent with standard roofing and merits a closer look to determine if it is a tarp, temporary covering, or non-roof structure. (2) A darker gray patch near the center of the upper roof plane that differs in tone from the surrounding shingle field — this could reflect moisture staining, an older repair, or simply a shadow artifact, but it is visible enough to note for in-person follow-up. No moss/growth, debris, or obvious large-scale missing shingles were observed. Overall condition is rated fair due to these two localized items that warrant ground-truth verification.
